status: DEV — NOT patient-facing; NOT clinical guidance. The unifying drug vocabulary: every name, synonym, brand, international and spelling variant that gets used interchangeably, linked to ONE identity. PRIMARY AUTHORITY is the PBS ingredient name (the Australian Government's own formulary) — NOT RxNorm's canonical, which is the USAN and would rename paracetamol to acetaminophen. RxNorm supplies the CONCEPT ID (RxCUI) only; WHO ATC codes come from PBS. Recording is not resolving: `usable_for_lookup` is false for anything ambiguous (a name reaching two drugs), for international variants (a US name must never steer an AU lookup) and for company-name artifacts. Nothing is binned — every name is kept and labelled, and the clinician decides what may steer. Records author to review_status 'draft'; canonicalise() will not read this until it is signed.

attestation.statement: Drug vocabulary built deterministically from PBS (AU authority: primary names, AU brands, ATC), RxNorm (concept id only), and the datastore's own names. The agent made no identity judgement of its own: every mapping is PBS's, RxNorm's or one this datastore already asserted, each recorded with its source so it can be checked. Requires clinician review before it may steer a lookup — a wrong vocabulary entry redirects a dose lookup to the wrong drug, which is why ambiguity is refused rather than guessed and why this is not self-approving.
attestation.scope: 1455 drugs · 5196 names (5107 steer · 73 ask-the-human · 16 refuse). Identity only — no dose, no clinical claim.

names.name: erythropoietin
names.source: breath-ezy datastore (also_known_as, E7 reconcile) — DISPOSITION OVERRIDDEN by clinician ruling (Kenneth Lee (MED0001857758), 2026-07-15): Clinician ruling on FL-34 vocabulary review, 2026-07-15: 'ask'. THIS OVERRIDES RxNORM, DELIBERATELY. RxNorm groups 'erythropoietin' under RxCUI 105694 — the same concept as epoetin alfa — so it is not a naming error the build could detect: by every mechanical test available, this alias is as sound as 'frusemide -> furosemide' (all 20 aliases share their primary's concept id; the check was run and found zero mismatches). Nor does the ambiguity detector fire, because the name reaches exactly one primary — nothing collides. RxNorm's grouping reflects US usage, where 'erythropoietin'/'EPO' commonly means epoetin alfa (Epogen/Procrit). In Australian practice it is a class term. That gap between a naming authority and bedside meaning is not derivable from the data; it takes a clinician, which is what clinical sign-off is for.
names.disposition_reason: ambiguous — 'erythropoietin' is the hormone/class term and this datastore holds four erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (epoetin alfa, epoetin lambda, darbepoetin alfa, methoxy polyethylene glycol-epoetin beta). The system does not choose between medications.
names.confirm_prompt: You entered "erythropoietin". In Australian practice that is the name of the hormone class, not of one medicine — it could mean epoetin alfa, epoetin lambda, darbepoetin alfa, or methoxy polyethylene glycol-epoetin beta, which are dosed differently. Which one do you mean?
